critique.summary: The article announces the release of Kitten TTS v0.8, an open-source, lightweight text-to-speech library with models ranging from 15M to 80M parameters and sizes between 25 MB and 80 MB. It highlights the library's CPU-optimized performance, multiple built-in voices, and features such as adjustable speech speed and text preprocessing.
mainPoints: Kitten TTS v0.8 offers three model sizes: 15M (nano), 40M (micro), and 80M (mini) parameters.
Models range in size from 25 MB (int8 quantized nano model) to 80 MB, suitable for edge deployment without requiring a GPU.
The library supports 8 built-in voices and outputs high-quality 24 kHz audio.
It is CPU-optimized using ONNX for efficient inference.
The project is open-source under the Apache 2.0 license and provides commercial support options.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Kitten TTS are consistent with publicly available information on the official GitHub repository and related documentation. The model sizes, parameter counts, and features such as CPU optimization and multiple voices are accurately described. The project is indeed open-source and uses ONNX for inference. The mention of commercial support and the license type also align with the repository details. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: User experience and audio quality comparisons with other TTS models are not discussed.
Potential limitations or issues, such as those reported with the int8 quantized nano model, are briefly mentioned but not elaborated.
The article does not provide independent benchmarks or third-party reviews of the model's performance.
Compatibility with various operating systems and integration complexity for developers is only briefly mentioned.
The roadmap and future development plans could impact the stability and features of the library.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the release of new small TTS models by KittenML, with users impressed by the size and quality improvements but raising questions about prosody, expressive control, and practical deployment scenarios. Several comments seek comparisons, technical details, and potential applications, while others inquire about GPU usage and platform support.

comments.author: magicalhippo
comments.text:
A lot of good small TTS models in recent times. Most seem to struggle hard on prosody though.

Kokoro TTS for example has a very good Norwegian voice but the rhythm and emphasizing is often so out of whack the generated speech is almost incomprehensible.

Haven't had time to check this model out yet, how does it fare here? What's needed to improve the models in this area now that the voice part is more or less solved?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced critique of TTS models focusing on prosody issues, which is a known challenge, making it a highly accurate and insightful observation.

comments.author: kevin42
comments.text:
What I love about OpenClaw is that I was able to send it a message on Discord with just this github URL and it started sending me voice messages using it within a few minutes. It also gave me a bunch of different benchmarks and sample audio.

I'm impressed with the quality given the size. I don't love the voices, but it's not bad. Running on an intel 9700 CPU, it's about 1.5x realtime using the 80M model. It wasn't any faster running on a 3080 GPU though.
comments.analysis: Provides firsthand usage experience with performance benchmarks and quality impressions, which adds practical value and credibility to the discussion.

comments.author: Remi_Etien
comments.text: 25MB is impressive. What's the tradeoff vs the 80M model — is it mainly voice quality or does it also affect pronunciation accuracy on less common words?
comments.analysis: Asks a relevant technical question about the tradeoffs between model sizes, showing an understanding of typical TTS model compromises.

comments.author: pumanoir
comments.text:
The example.py file says "it will run blazing fast on any GPU. But this example will run on CPU."

I couldn't locate how to run it on a GPU anywhere in the repo.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about documentation clarity and GPU support, which is important for users aiming to optimize performance.

comments.author: altruios
comments.text:
One of the core features I look for is expressive control.

Either in the form of the api via pitch/speed/volume controls, for more deterministic controls.

Or in expressive tags such as [coughs], [urgently], or [laughs in melodic ascending and descending arpeggiated gibberish babbles].

the 25MB model is amazingly good for being 25MB. How does it handle expressive tags?
comments.analysis: Highlights an important aspect of TTS usability—expressive control—and praises the model size, contributing useful feedback and feature requests.

comments.author: ks2048
comments.text:
There's a number of recent, good quality, small TTS models.

If the author doesn't describe some detail about the data, training, or a novel architecture, etc, I only assume they just took another one, do a little finetuning, and repackage as a new product.
comments.analysis: Offers a skeptical but reasonable perspective on the originality of the models, emphasizing the need for transparency about methodology.

comments.author: janice1999
comments.text: What's the actual install size for a working example? Like similar "tiny" projects, do these models actually require installing 1GB+ of dependencies?
comments.analysis: Raises a practical concern about dependency bloat, which is a common issue in deploying small models effectively.

comments.author: armcat
comments.text: This is awesome, well done. Been doing lot of work with voice assistants, if you can replicate voice cloning Qwen3-TTS into this small factor, you will be absolute legends!
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and suggests a valuable direction for future development, though speculative about feasibility.

comments.author: DavidTompkins
comments.text: This would be great as a js package - 25mb is small enough that I think it'd be worth it (in-browser tts is still pretty bad and varies by browser)
comments.analysis: Offers a practical suggestion for expanding accessibility and usability, reflecting awareness of current browser TTS limitations.

comments.author: daneel_w
comments.text: A very clear improvement from the first set of models you released some time ago. I'm really impressed. Thanks for sharing it all.
comments.analysis: Provides positive feedback based on prior experience, though lacking technical detail.
